ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic fried rice class (FOODON_00002694). No specific code exists for shredded pork and egg fried rice; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers fried rice as a category. No specific facet for shredded pork and egg variant; closest_parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for fried rice with shredded pork and eggs.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of fried rice with shredded pork and eggs is approximately 350 g, equivalent to one plate as typically served at a Chinese restaurant such as Din Tai Fung.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Rice, with Meat and Egg — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA SR Legacy composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo reports nutrient values for fried rice with egg and meat (generic Chinese-style). Values adapted to reflect shredded pork and egg variant using HPB Foodo fried rice with meat and egg entry as primary anchor, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked white rice (NDB 20444), pork loin cooked (NDB 10062), and whole egg cooked (NDB 01129). Per-100g values represent a weighted composite consistent with HPB Foodo reported range for this dish category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Fried rice (white rice, stir-fried with egg and soy sauce)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for shredded pork and egg fried rice. GI of 55 assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice stir-fried with egg and soy sauce (GI ~54–57), which is the closest analogous food. According to standard classification, a GI of 55 is considered 'low'. The addition of shredded pork does not materially alter the GI relative to the rice-dominant carbohydrate matrix. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 47.1, where carbohydrate per serving = 24.5 g/100g × 350 g serving = 85.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (equating to ~1330 mg per standard 350 g serving), this dish contributes more than half the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals and should be consumed infrequently or with portion reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 55 (low) and a glycemic load of approximately 47 per standard serving, this dish can still cause a significant postprandial glucose rise due to the large portion size; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat and high sodium from soy sauce seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of 1.5 g saturated fat per 100 g and high sodium (~380 mg/100 g) warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease; choosing smaller portions and requesting reduced soy sauce can help limit both saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Rice with Shredded Pork and Eggs (肉丝蛋炒饭, Ròu sī dàn chǎo fàn) is a classic Chinese stir-fried rice dish prepared by wok-frying cooked white rice with thinly shredded pork, beaten eggs, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spring onions over high heat. The dish is a staple of Chinese home cooking and restaurant menus across China and Singapore, and is a signature item at Din Tai Fung. The texture is characteristically loose and slightly chewy, with individual rice grains coated in egg and seasoning, interspersed with tender strips of pork. The flavour profile is savoury and umami-rich, with a subtle smokiness from wok hei. Nutritionally, a standard 350 g restaurant serving provides approximately 612 kcal, 25 g protein, 20 g fat, and 86 g carbohydrate. The dish has a low glycemic index (~55) but a high glycemic load per serving (~47) due to the large portion size, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce, presenting a concern for hypertensive individuals. The dish contains eggs and soy allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal dietary requirements due to the pork content.

confidence_reason: No exact nutrient entry for Din Tai Fung shredded pork and egg fried rice was found in HPB Foodo or USDA databases. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried rice with meat and egg (generic Chinese-style) as the primary anchor, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data. The adaptation is defensible and internally consistent but does not constitute an exact match to this specific restaurant preparation.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source entries used for this dish. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the primary source entries. Trans fat is reported as 0.0; this is confirmed as negligible/trace in the source data for this dish type. GI of 55 (low category) is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice stir-fried with egg and soy sauce; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 85.75) / 100 ≈ 47.1 g, based on 24.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a 350 g serving size. This dish contains pork and is not halal; the is_halal flag is set to false. The restaurant source (Din Tai Fung, Marina Bay Link Mall, field survey data) is noted for provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.
